- Describe who I am and what I do
- Set my default communication style and format
- Tell Claude what to never do when working with me
- Define the tone I want in every response
- Include any default behaviors I would want in every session

Write them in second person, as if Claude is reading rules about how to help me. Be specific. No generic advice. Under 400 words.
Take the output and paste it into your Project Instructions. This becomes Claude's permanent operating mode for every conversation in this Project.
Claude Is Not What You Think

4 - Claude is not a search engine
Most people use Claude the way they use Google. They type a question and wait for an answer. That is the lowest-value way to use it.
Claude is not a retrieval tool. It is a thinking partner. It doesn't just pull information, it reasons, synthesizes, argues, and builds on context. The moment you treat it like a search engine, you cut its usefulness by 80 percent.
Stop asking Claude what something is. Start asking Claude to help you think through something.

Instead of: "What is prompt caching?"
Try: "I'm building a workflow that calls Claude 20 times per session. Walk me through how prompt caching works and whether it would actually reduce my costs given that context."
The second prompt gives Claude a problem to solve with you. The first gives it a definition to recite.
5 - Ask Claude to ask you questions first
This is one of the most powerful techniques almost nobody uses. Before Claude starts any complex task, tell it to gather information from you first.
When Claude asks you questions before starting, the output is dramatically better because it's built on the right foundation. Without this, Claude makes assumptions and you spend time correcting things that could have been right the first time.
Use this before any important task:
Before you start, ask me the 5 most important questions that would help you do this well. 

After I answer, then begin.
Or for a specific task:
I need you to help me write a cold email to a potential client. 

Before you write anything, ask me what you need to know to make this genuinely good, not generic.
What Even Regular Users Don't Know

6 - Style cloning

When Claude writes in your voice without examples, it writes in its own voice. The output is grammatically correct and completely wrong in tone. It sounds like AI because it is.
Give Claude three to five samples of your own writing. Ask it to analyze your patterns, not just describe your style. After that analysis, it writes like you, not like a polished corporate assistant.
Here are 3 examples of my writing:

[paste sample 1]
[paste sample 2]
[paste sample 3]

Analyze my writing style in detail. Look at: sentence length, rhythm, vocabulary choices, how I open and close paragraphs, what I avoid, how formal or informal I am, and any patterns that make my writing distinct.

After this, when I ask you to write anything for me, match this style exactly. Do not default to your own patterns.
7 - Claude as your sparring partner
Most people ask Claude to help them with ideas. That means Claude builds on what you say, adds to it, expands it. You get agreement and elaboration.
That is useful sometimes. But it is not how you stress-test an idea.
Before committing to any plan, decision, or piece of writing, ask Claude to attack it. Not critique it. Attack it. The distinction matters.
Here is my plan: [describe your plan]

Your job is to destroy it. Find every assumption I'm making that could be wrong. Find every way this could fail. Argue the opposite position as hard as you can. Do not be polite. Do not add qualifications. Just attack.

After that, steelman my position. Build the strongest possible case for why I'm right.

Then tell me what you actually think.
8 - Extended Thinking
Most Claude users have never turned this on. Extended Thinking is a mode where Claude reasons through a problem step by step before giving you an answer, instead of going straight to the output.
For simple tasks, you don't need it. For complex decisions, analysis, or any question where you want Claude to actually think rather than pattern-match, turn it on.
In Claude, click the brain icon before sending your message. Or add this to your prompt:
Think through this carefully before responding. 

Work through the problem step by step, show your reasoning, identify where you're uncertain, then give me your conclusion.
The difference in output quality on hard questions is significant.
9 - Claude writes prompts for Claude
This is the most underused thing you can do. If you're not sure how to prompt Claude for a specific task, ask Claude to write the prompt for you.
Claude knows what kinds of instructions produce better results. Let it use that knowledge on your behalf.
I need Claude to help me [describe your actual task]. 

Write me the best possible prompt for this task. 

Include role, context, format instructions, and any constraints that would improve the output. 

Then use that prompt immediately.
How to Spend Fewer Tokens and Get More

10 - Specify the output length
Claude's default is to write as much as it thinks is appropriate. That is usually more than you need, which means more tokens used, more time spent reading, and more noise in the output.
Tell Claude exactly how long you want the answer before it starts.
Answer in 3 sentences maximum.
Give me 5 bullet points. No explanations. Just the points.
Write this in under 150 words.
This one instruction cuts token usage on most tasks by 40 to 60 percent without losing any of the value you actually need.
11 - Remove the preamble
Every Claude response defaults to starting with something you didn't ask for. "Great question. Let me break this down for you." Or a full restatement of what you just said. Or a disclaimer. Or a closing summary that repeats everything it just told you.

You didn't ask for any of that. It costs tokens and it wastes your time.
Add this to your Custom Instructions:
Never start responses with preamble, affirmations, or restatements of my question. 

Go directly to the answer. 

Do not add a summary at the end unless I specifically ask for one. 

No disclaimers unless the topic genuinely requires one.
12 - Don't re-explain yourself every conversation
If you're pasting the same background information into every new chat, you are wasting tokens every single time and training yourself into a habit that costs you more as Claude usage scales.
This is exactly what Projects and Custom Instructions are for. Put your context in once. Let Claude read it automatically at the start of every session. Never paste your background again.
If you are not using Projects yet, start there before anything else in this article.
13 - Start a new chat for a new topic
Claude carries the context of everything said earlier in a conversation. When you switch topics inside a long chat, Claude still has all the previous context loaded. That means more tokens used on every response, slower processing, and context bleed from earlier in the conversation affecting your new topic.
When you switch to something unrelated, start a fresh chat inside your Project. You keep the Project memory. You lose the irrelevant baggage.
Ready to Use Right Now

These are complete prompts you can copy and use immediately.
14 - Understand anything through analogies (Feynman method)
Most explanations Claude gives by default are technically correct and practically useless. They use the same vocabulary as the thing you're trying to understand, which means you walk away with a definition but not actual comprehension.
The Feynman method forces understanding through simplicity. If Claude can't explain it in plain terms using analogies, it means the explanation isn't clear enough yet. This prompt works for anything from investing to quantum physics to how a specific API works.
Explain [topic] to me using only analogies and everyday examples. No jargon. Assume I have no background in this field.

After each analogy, check whether I've actually understood it by asking me one question. Based on my answer, go deeper or adjust the explanation.

Keep going until I can explain it back to you in my own words without using any technical terms.
15 - Travel plan built around how you actually travel
Most travel planning starts with destinations and ends with a generic itinerary you could find on any travel blog. Claude can do something different: build a plan around your specific travel style, pace, budget, and what actually matters to you, not what's on every must-see list.
The key is giving it real information about you, not just dates and locations.
I'm planning a trip to [destination]. I'll be there for [number of days]. My budget is approximately [amount] per day including accommodation.

Here's how I actually travel: [describe your style — do you like slow mornings or packed days, touristy places or local spots, museums or food, active or relaxed, solo or with someone, etc].

Things I want to avoid: [crowds, tourist traps, expensive restaurants, long transport times, etc].

Build me a day-by-day itinerary that fits this. For each day, include where to stay, what to do, where to eat, and any logistics I need to know. Flag anything that requires booking in advance.
16 - Monthly expense analysis with real conclusions
Most people look at their bank statement and feel vaguely bad about their spending without understanding what's actually happening. Claude can turn raw numbers into a clear picture of where your money is going and what you should actually do about it.
This works best when you paste real data, not estimates.
I'm going to paste my expenses from the last month. Analyze them and tell me:

1. What categories am I spending the most on
2. Where my spending looks unusual compared to what I described as my goals
3. What I could cut without meaningfully affecting my life
4. What I'm probably underspending on that matters
5. One specific change that would have the biggest financial impact

Here are my expenses: [paste your bank statement or expense list]

My financial goals right now: [describe what you're trying to do — save more, pay off debt, invest, etc]
17 - Claude as your personal thinking partner
Most people don't have someone in their life who will listen without judgment, ask the right questions, and help them work through something they're stuck on without pushing their own agenda. Claude can fill that role, but only if you give it the right instructions.
This isn't therapy. It's structured self-reflection with an outside perspective that helps you think more clearly.
I want to talk through something I'm dealing with. Your job is not to give me advice right away.

First, ask me questions to understand the situation fully. What's actually happening, how I feel about it, what I've already tried, and what outcome I'm hoping for.

After you understand the full picture, reflect back what you're hearing — not just the facts but what seems to be underneath them.

Then, and only then, offer your perspective. Be honest, not reassuring. Tell me what you actually think, including anything I might not want to hear.

Here's what's on my mind: [describe what you want to think through]
18 - Stress-test any business idea before you commit
Most business ideas die because people fall in love with them before testing them. They spend months building something nobody wants because they never honestly asked whether the idea was actually good.
Claude can act as a ruthless first filter. Not to kill ideas, but to find the real problems before they cost you time and money.
I have a business idea I want to stress-test before I invest serious time in it.

Here's the idea: [describe it in detail — what it is, who it's for, how it makes money, why you think it works]

Your job is to find everything wrong with it. Specifically:

1. What assumptions am I making that could be wrong
2. Who already does this and why I might lose to them
3. Why the target customer might not actually pay for this
4. What would have to be true for this to work, and how likely is that
5. The single biggest problem with this idea

Be specific. Generic risks like "the market might not be ready" are not useful. Give me the real version of each problem.

After that, tell me what the idea would need to look like to actually work.
The Actual Point

Claude is not smarter than you. It does not have better ideas than you. What it has is infinite patience, broad knowledge, and the ability to think through problems from angles you haven't considered.
The people who get the most from Claude are not the ones with the best questions. They are the ones who have set it up to understand them, who give it real context, and who know how to use it as a partner rather than a dispenser.
Most people will read this and keep opening Claude the same way they always have.
Set it up once. Change how you work permanently.
Want to publish your own Article?
Upgrade to Premium

After that, steelman my position. Build the strongest possible case for why I'm right.

Then tell me what you actually think.
8 - Extended Thinking
Most Claude users have never turned this on. Extended Thinking is a mode where Claude reasons through a problem step by step before giving you an answer, instead of going straight to the output.
For simple tasks, you don't need it. For complex decisions, analysis, or any question where you want Claude to actually think rather than pattern-match, turn it on.
In Claude, click the brain icon before sending your message. Or add this to your prompt:
Think through this carefully before responding. 

Work through the problem step by step, show your reasoning, identify where you're uncertain, then give me your conclusion.
The difference in output quality on hard questions is significant.
9 - Claude writes prompts for Claude
This is the most underused thing you can do. If you're not sure how to prompt Claude for a specific task, ask Claude to write the prompt for you.
Claude knows what kinds of instructions produce better results. Let it use that knowledge on your behalf.
I need Claude to help me [describe your actual task]. 

Write me the best possible prompt for this task. 

Include role, context, format instructions, and any constraints that would improve the output. 

Then use that prompt immediately.
How to Spend Fewer Tokens and Get More

10 - Specify the output length
Claude's default is to write as much as it thinks is appropriate. That is usually more than you need, which means more tokens used, more time spent reading, and more noise in the output.
Tell Claude exactly how long you want the answer before it starts.
Answer in 3 sentences maximum.
Give me 5 bullet points. No explanations. Just the points.
Write this in under 150 words.
This one instruction cuts token usage on most tasks by 40 to 60 percent without losing any of the value you actually need.
11 - Remove the preamble
Every Claude response defaults to starting with something you didn't ask for. "Great question. Let me break this down for you." Or a full restatement of what you just said. Or a disclaimer. Or a closing summary that repeats everything it just told you.

You didn't ask for any of that. It costs tokens and it wastes your time.
Add this to your Custom Instructions:
Never start responses with preamble, affirmations, or restatements of my question. 

Go directly to the answer. 

Do not add a summary at the end unless I specifically ask for one. 

No disclaimers unless the topic genuinely requires one.
12 - Don't re-explain yourself every conversation
If you're pasting the same background information into every new chat, you are wasting tokens every single time and training yourself into a habit that costs you more as Claude usage scales.
This is exactly what Projects and Custom Instructions are for. Put your context in once. Let Claude read it automatically at the start of every session. Never paste your background again.
If you are not using Projects yet, start there before anything else in this article.
13 - Start a new chat for a new topic
Claude carries the context of everything said earlier in a conversation. When you switch topics inside a long chat, Claude still has all the previous context loaded. That means more tokens used on every response, slower processing, and context bleed from earlier in the conversation affecting your new topic.
When you switch to something unrelated, start a fresh chat inside your Project. You keep the Project memory. You lose the irrelevant baggage.
Ready to Use Right Now

These are complete prompts you can copy and use immediately.
14 - Understand anything through analogies (Feynman method)
Most explanations Claude gives by default are technically correct and practically useless. They use the same vocabulary as the thing you're trying to understand, which means you walk away with a definition but not actual comprehension.
The Feynman method forces understanding through simplicity. If Claude can't explain it in plain terms using analogies, it means the explanation isn't clear enough yet. This prompt works for anything from investing to quantum physics to how a specific API works.
Explain [topic] to me using only analogies and everyday examples. No jargon. Assume I have no background in this field.

After each analogy, check whether I've actually understood it by asking me one question. Based on my answer, go deeper or adjust the explanation.

Keep going until I can explain it back to you in my own words without using any technical terms.
15 - Travel plan built around how you actually travel
Most travel planning starts with destinations and ends with a generic itinerary you could find on any travel blog. Claude can do something different: build a plan around your specific travel style, pace, budget, and what actually matters to you, not what's on every must-see list.
The key is giving it real information about you, not just dates and locations.
I'm planning a trip to [destination]. I'll be there for [number of days]. My budget is approximately [amount] per day including accommodation.

Here's how I actually travel: [describe your style — do you like slow mornings or packed days, touristy places or local spots, museums or food, active or relaxed, solo or with someone, etc].

Things I want to avoid: [crowds, tourist traps, expensive restaurants, long transport times, etc].

Build me a day-by-day itinerary that fits this. For each day, include where to stay, what to do, where to eat, and any logistics I need to know. Flag anything that requires booking in advance.
16 - Monthly expense analysis with real conclusions
Most people look at their bank statement and feel vaguely bad about their spending without understanding what's actually happening. Claude can turn raw numbers into a clear picture of where your money is going and what you should actually do about it.
This works best when you paste real data, not estimates.
I'm going to paste my expenses from the last month. Analyze them and tell me:

1. What categories am I spending the most on
2. Where my spending looks unusual compared to what I described as my goals
3. What I could cut without meaningfully affecting my life
4. What I'm probably underspending on that matters
5. One specific change that would have the biggest financial impact

Here are my expenses: [paste your bank statement or expense list]

My financial goals right now: [describe what you're trying to do — save more, pay off debt, invest, etc]
17 - Claude as your personal thinking partner
Most people don't have someone in their life who will listen without judgment, ask the right questions, and help them work through something they're stuck on without pushing their own agenda. Claude can fill that role, but only if you give it the right instructions.
This isn't therapy. It's structured self-reflection with an outside perspective that helps you think more clearly.
I want to talk through something I'm dealing with. Your job is not to give me advice right away.

First, ask me questions to understand the situation fully. What's actually happening, how I feel about it, what I've already tried, and what outcome I'm hoping for.

After you understand the full picture, reflect back what you're hearing — not just the facts but what seems to be underneath them.

Then, and only then, offer your perspective. Be honest, not reassuring. Tell me what you actually think, including anything I might not want to hear.

Here's what's on my mind: [describe what you want to think through]
18 - Stress-test any business idea before you commit
Most business ideas die because people fall in love with them before testing them. They spend months building something nobody wants because they never honestly asked whether the idea was actually good.
Claude can act as a ruthless first filter. Not to kill ideas, but to find the real problems before they cost you time and money.
I have a business idea I want to stress-test before I invest serious time in it.

Here's the idea: [describe it in detail — what it is, who it's for, how it makes money, why you think it works]

Your job is to find everything wrong with it. Specifically:

1. What assumptions am I making that could be wrong
2. Who already does this and why I might lose to them
3. Why the target customer might not actually pay for this
4. What would have to be true for this to work, and how likely is that
5. The single biggest problem with this idea

Be specific. Generic risks like "the market might not be ready" are not useful. Give me the real version of each problem.

After that, tell me what the idea would need to look like to actually work.
The Actual Point

Claude is not smarter than you. It does not have better ideas than you. What it has is infinite patience, broad knowledge, and the ability to think through problems from angles you haven't considered.
The people who get the most from Claude are not the ones with the best questions. They are the ones who have set it up to understand them, who give it real context, and who know how to use it as a partner rather than a dispenser.
Most people will read this and keep opening Claude the same way they always have.
Set it up once. Change how you work permanently.

Claude has been out for two years. Most people who use it every day are still using 10% of what it can do.
Not because it's complicated. Because nobody showed them what the other 90% looks like.

This guide fixes that. By the end you will have Claude set up in a way that remembers you, understands you, and works the way you actually think. And you will know how to use it for things most people have never tried.
Start Here

1 - Create a Project, not a chat

Every time you open a new Claude chat, it starts with zero memory. It doesn't know your name, your work, your goals, or how you like to communicate. You spend the first few messages re-explaining yourself, or you don't, and Claude gives you something generic that doesn't fit how you actually work.

Projects fix this. A Project is a persistent workspace where Claude keeps context across every conversation inside it. You set it up once and every session that follows starts with Claude already knowing who you are.

Go to Claude, click Projects in the sidebar, and create a new one. Name it something like "Work" or "Personal" depending on how you plan to use it. Everything that follows goes inside this Project.
2 - Tell Claude who you are

Before Claude can help you well, it needs to understand you. Most people skip this step entirely and wonder why Claude gives them answers that feel slightly off.

Paste this into your Project and fill in every field honestly. The more specific you are, the better every single response becomes.
My name is [your name].

I work as [your role or profession]. My main responsibilities are [2-3 things you actually do day to day].

Right now my biggest goals are [1-3 specific goals you're working toward].

I use Claude mostly for [list your main use cases — writing, research, analysis, learning, coding, etc].

My background and knowledge level: [what you know well, what you're learning, what you're new to].

How I like to receive information: [e.g. direct and concise / detailed with examples / step by step / no bullet points / short paragraphs].

Things I don't want: [e.g. don't add disclaimers, don't use corporate language, don't repeat what I just said back to me, don't start with "Great question"].

Topics and areas I care about: [your interests, industry, niche].
Save this inside your Project's knowledge base. Claude will read it at the start of every conversation in this Project.
3 - Turn that into Custom Instructions

Pasting your background is a good start. But Custom Instructions go further. They tell Claude not just who you are, but exactly how to behave with you by default.

Paste this prompt into Claude after you've filled in the template above:
Based on everything I've told you about myself, write me a set of custom instructions for this Claude Project.

The instructions should:
- Describe who I am and what I do
- Set my default communication style and format
- Tell Claude what to never do when working with me
- Define the tone I want in every response
- Include any default behaviors I would want in every session

Write them in second person, as if Claude is reading rules about how to help me. Be specific. No generic advice. Under 400 words.
Take the output and paste it into your Project Instructions. This becomes Claude's permanent operating mode for every conversation in this Project.
Claude Is Not What You Think

4 - Claude is not a search engine
Most people use Claude the way they use Google. They type a question and wait for an answer. That is the lowest-value way to use it.
Claude is not a retrieval tool. It is a thinking partner. It doesn't just pull information, it reasons, synthesizes, argues, and builds on context. The moment you treat it like a search engine, you cut its usefulness by 80 percent.
Stop asking Claude what something is. Start asking Claude to help you think through something.

Instead of: "What is prompt caching?"
Try: "I'm building a workflow that calls Claude 20 times per session. Walk me through how prompt caching works and whether it would actually reduce my costs given that context."
The second prompt gives Claude a problem to solve with you. The first gives it a definition to recite.
5 - Ask Claude to ask you questions first
This is one of the most powerful techniques almost nobody uses. Before Claude starts any complex task, tell it to gather information from you first.
When Claude asks you questions before starting, the output is dramatically better because it's built on the right foundation. Without this, Claude makes assumptions and you spend time correcting things that could have been right the first time.
Use this before any important task:
Before you start, ask me the 5 most important questions that would help you do this well. 

After I answer, then begin.
Or for a specific task:
I need you to help me write a cold email to a potential client. 

Before you write anything, ask me what you need to know to make this genuinely good, not generic.
What Even Regular Users Don't Know

6 - Style cloning

When Claude writes in your voice without examples, it writes in its own voice. The output is grammatically correct and completely wrong in tone. It sounds like AI because it is.
Give Claude three to five samples of your own writing. Ask it to analyze your patterns, not just describe your style. After that analysis, it writes like you, not like a polished corporate assistant.
Here are 3 examples of my writing:

[paste sample 1]
[paste sample 2]
[paste sample 3]

Analyze my writing style in detail. Look at: sentence length, rhythm, vocabulary choices, how I open and close paragraphs, what I avoid, how formal or informal I am, and any patterns that make my writing distinct.

After this, when I ask you to write anything for me, match this style exactly. Do not default to your own patterns.

Most people ask Claude to help them with ideas. That means Claude builds on what you say, adds to it, expands it. You get agreement and elaboration.

title: Use **Google Apps Script + Classroom API + Drive API + Gemini/ChatGPT marking API**.
content:
[Fri, May 15, 2026, 05:46 AM]


## Recommended workflow

Use **Google Apps Script + Classroom API + Drive API + Gemini/ChatGPT marking API**.

Google Classroom API can list student submissions and update grades. Google’s guide says grades can be saved as `draftGrade`, or as both `draftGrade` and `assignedGrade` when returning marks. ([Google for Developers][1]) Drive/App Script can create folders, copy files, and rename them. ([Google for Developers][2])

## Step-by-step system

### 1. Create one master folder

Example:

```text
Google Classroom Marking/
 └── Course Name/
     └── Assignment Topic/
         ├── Assignments/
         ├── Student Scripts/
         ├── Rubrics/
         ├── Marked Reports/
         └── Marks CSV/
```

### 2. Pull files from Google Classroom

For each assignment:

1. Select `courseId`
2. Select `courseWorkId`
3. Get all `studentSubmissions`
4. Read each student’s attached files
5. Copy each file into your Drive folder

Classroom API supports listing student submissions. ([Google for Developers][3])

### 3. Rename files automatically

Use this format:

```text
Topic_Title - Student Name - Script.pdf
Topic_Title - Assignment.pdf
Topic_Title - Student Name - Marked Report.pdf
Topic_Title - Rubric.pdf
```

Example:

```text
Reggae History - Brithany Smith - Script.pdf
Reggae History - Assignment.pdf
Reggae History - Brithany Smith - Marked Report.pdf
```

### 4. Create or load the rubric

The rubric should include:

| Item            | Description              |
| --------------- | ------------------------ |
| Question number | Q1, Q2, Q3               |
| Max marks       | Total available          |
| Marks gained    | What the student earned  |
| Marks lost      | What was missing/wrong   |
| Error noted     | Specific mistake         |
| Correction      | Correct answer or method |
| Feedback        | Short comment            |

### 5. Mark each student script

For each script, the marking agent should produce:

```text
Student Name:
Topic:
Assignment:
Total Marks:
Percentage:

Question-by-question marking:
- Marks gained
- Marks lost
- Errors
- Corrections
- Teacher feedback
```

### 6. Generate a marked report

Create one PDF/report per student:

```text
Marked Report
Student: Brithany Smith
Topic: Reggae History
Total Score: 12/15
Percentage: 80%

Rubric Table
Errors and Corrections
Teacher Feedback
```

### 7. Save the marks in a spreadsheet

Columns:

| Student Name | Topic | Script File | Score | Total | Percentage | Report Link | Classroom Status |
| ------------ | ----: | ----------- | ----: | ----: | ---------: | ----------- | ---------------- |

### 8. Send marks back to Google Classroom

Use Classroom API to update:

```text
draftGrade
assignedGrade
```

Save as `draftGrade` first if you want to review before students see it. Use both `draftGrade` and `assignedGrade` when you are ready to return the grade. ([Google for Developers][1])

## Best build order

Do it in this order:

1. **Export files from Classroom**
2. **Rename and organise files in Drive**
3. **Create marks spreadsheet**
4. **Create rubric template**
5. **Mark one student script**
6. **Generate one marked report**
7. **Repeat for all students**
8. **Push marks back to Classroom**
9. **Add a teacher review step before final return**

## Important warning

Do **not** send marks straight back automatically at first. Use this safer flow:

```text
AI marks script → Teacher reviews → Approve → Send grade to Classroom
```

This avoids wrong marks being returned to students.

[1]: https://developers.google.com/workspace/classroom/guides/classroom-api/manage-grades?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Set & update grades | Google Classroom"
[2]: https://developers.google.com/apps-script/reference/drive?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Drive Service | Apps Script"
[3]: https://developers.google.com/workspace/classroom/reference/rest?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Google Classroom API"


Classroom Marking
 └── Assignment Topic
     ├── Assignment
     ├── Scripts
     ├── Rubrics
     ├── Marked Reports
     └── Marks Spreadsheet

title: Step-by-step: Test Whether Your Google Classroom Setup Will Work
content:
• # Step-by-step: Test Whether Your Google Classroom Setup Will Work

We will test this in stages so you can identify exactly what your university has disabled.

---

# Stage 1 — Check Basic Google Classroom Access

## Step 1: Open Google Classroom

Open:

[Google Classroom](https://classroom.google.com?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Check whether you can:

* Open your class
* Open assignments
* View student submissions
* Download attachments

---

## Step 2: Check if you are a Teacher

Inside the classroom:

1. Open your course
2. Click **People**
3. Confirm you appear under:

```text
Teachers
```

If you are only listed as a student, the automation will not work properly.

---

# Stage 2 — Check Whether Google Drive Access Works

Your system depends heavily on Drive.

## Test

Open:

[Google Drive](https://drive.google.com?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Create a folder:

```text
Classroom Automation Test
```

Inside it create:

```text
Scripts
Assignments
Reports
```

Now test whether you can:

* Upload a PDF
* Rename the PDF
* Move the PDF between folders

If all works → Drive permissions are good.

---

# Stage 3 — Test Whether Classroom Attachments Can Be Accessed

## Step 1

Open one assignment.

## Step 2

Open one student submission.

## Step 3

Try to:

* Preview the file
* Download the file
* Open in Drive

### Results

| Result               | Meaning                 |
| -------------------- | ----------------------- |
| Works                | Good                    |
| Download blocked     | University restrictions |
| Cannot open in Drive | API may fail later      |

---

# Stage 4 — Check if Apps Script Is Allowed

This is VERY important.

## Step 1

Open:

[Google Apps Script](https://script.google.com?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

## Step 2

Create a new project.

Rename it:

```text
Classroom Test
```

---

## Step 3 — Run a Simple Test Script

Paste this:

```javascript
function testDrive() {
  const folders = DriveApp.getFolders();
  
  while (folders.hasNext()) {
    Logger.log(folders.next().getName());
  }
}
```

Click:

```text
Run
```

---

## What Happens?

### GOOD

If Google asks:

```text
Review Permissions
Allow
```

then Apps Script is enabled.

### BAD

If you see:

```text
Access denied by administrator
Service disabled
Restricted by organisation
```

your university has blocked Apps Script access.

---

# Stage 5 — Check if Classroom API Is Disabled

This is the most important test.

---

## Step 1

In Apps Script:

### Click

```text
Services (+)
```

on the left sidebar.

---

## Step 2

Search for:

```text
Classroom API
```

---

## Results

| Result                | Meaning                  |
| --------------------- | ------------------------ |
| Classroom API appears | Excellent                |
| Cannot add service    | Restricted               |
| Admin policy block    | University disabled APIs |

---

# Stage 6 — Run a Classroom Read Test

If Classroom API was available:

Paste this:

```javascript
function testClassroom() {
  const courses = Classroom.Courses.list();
  Logger.log(courses);
}
```

Run it.

---

## Results

### GOOD

You see course information in logs.

### BAD

Possible errors:

```text
Not Authorized
Admin policy enforced
Classroom API disabled
```

---

# Stage 7 — Check Whether You Can Return Grades

This is critical for automation.

## Manual Test

Inside Classroom:

1. Open an assignment
2. Enter a draft grade
3. Return the assignment

---

## Results

| Result            | Meaning                 |
| ----------------- | ----------------------- |
| Can return grades | API likely usable       |
| Return disabled   | Institution restriction |

---

# Most Common University Restrictions

Universities often disable:

| Feature            | Impact                    |
| ------------------ | ------------------------- |
| Apps Script        | Stops automation          |
| Classroom API      | Stops reading submissions |
| Drive API          | Stops file copying        |
| External AI access | Stops Gemini/OpenAI       |
| OAuth approval     | Blocks login permissions  |

---

# If Your University Blocks APIs

You still have options:

## Option 1 — Personal Google Account (Recommended)

Create:

```text
Personal Gmail
```

Then:

* Create shared Drive folders
* Export Classroom files manually
* Run automation on personal account

This is usually the easiest path.

---

## Option 2 — Use Google Takeout/Downloads

Manual workflow:

```text
Download submissions
→ Upload to automation folder
→ AI marks
→ Upload marks manually
```

---

# Best First Test

Do ONLY these today:

## Test Checklist

### 1. Can you open Apps Script?

[Apps Script](https://script.google.com?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### 2. Can you add Classroom API service?

### 3. Can this script run?

```javascript
function testClassroom() {
  const courses = Classroom.Courses.list();
  Logger.log(courses);
}
```

Those three tests will tell you almost everything.

---

# Expected Time

| Task                  |      Time |
| --------------------- | --------: |
| Basic tests           | 20–30 min |
| Apps Script setup     |    30 min |
| API verification      |    15 min |
| First automation test | 1–2 hours |

If you want next, I can also walk you through:

1. **The exact Apps Script clicks**
2. **How to enable APIs**
3. **How to build the first automatic file renamer**
4. **How to test one real student submission**
5. **How to create the marking spreadsheet automatically**

| ----------------- | ----------------------- |
| Can return grades | API likely usable       |
| Return disabled   | Institution restriction |

---

# Most Common University Restrictions

Universities often disable:

| Feature            | Impact                    |
| ------------------ | ------------------------- |
| Apps Script        | Stops automation          |
| Classroom API      | Stops reading submissions |
| Drive API          | Stops file copying        |
| External AI access | Stops Gemini/OpenAI       |
| OAuth approval     | Blocks login permissions  |

---

# If Your University Blocks APIs

You still have options:

## Option 1 — Personal Google Account (Recommended)

Create:

```text
Personal Gmail
```

Then:

* Create shared Drive folders
* Export Classroom files manually
* Run automation on personal account

This is usually the easiest path.

---

## Option 2 — Use Google Takeout/Downloads

Manual workflow:

```text
Download submissions
→ Upload to automation folder
→ AI marks
→ Upload marks manually
```

---

# Best First Test

Do ONLY these today:

## Test Checklist

### 1. Can you open Apps Script?

[Apps Script](https://script.google.com?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### 2. Can you add Classroom API service?

### 3. Can this script run?

```javascript
function testClassroom() {
  const courses = Classroom.Courses.list();
  Logger.log(courses);
}
```

Those three tests will tell you almost everything.

---

# Expected Time

| Task                  |      Time |
| --------------------- | --------: |
| Basic tests           | 20–30 min |
| Apps Script setup     |    30 min |
| API verification      |    15 min |
| First automation test | 1–2 hours |

If you want next, I can also walk you through:

1. **The exact Apps Script clicks**
2. **How to enable APIs**
3. **How to build the first automatic file renamer**
4. **How to test one real student submission**
5. **How to create the marking spreadsheet automatically**

title: Integrate n8n as the automation layer
content:
[Fri, May 15, 2026 at 01:58 PM]

Yes — integrate n8n as the automation layer, not as the main app.

Recommended app structure

Your App / Bolt / Supabase
        ↓
Webhook trigger
        ↓
n8n workflow
        ↓
Google Drive / Classroom / Gemini / ChatGPT / PDF generation
        ↓
Send result back to your app

Best first workflow to build

AI Marking Workflow

1. User uploads:
    * assessment paper
    * student script
    * rubric
2. Your app sends file links to n8n webhook
3. n8n does:
    * save files to Google Drive
    * send script to Gemini OCR
    * send extracted text + rubric to marking agent
    * send marked result to QC agent
    * calculate total score and percentage
    * generate PDF/LaTeX report
    * send mark back to Google Classroom
4. n8n returns status to your app:
    * Preparing Documents
    * Extracting Evidence
    * Marking Script
    * Quality Control
    * Report Generation
    * Complete

n8n supports webhooks, API calls, Google integrations, and self-hosting/cloud workflows, so it fits this role well.  ￼

Start simple

Build only this first:

App button → n8n webhook → Google Drive folder → return success message

Once that works, add OCR, marking, QC, PDF, then Classroom marks.

Important setup

For Google integrations, n8n will need proper OAuth setup. If self-hosted, webhook and OAuth URLs must be configured correctly, especially WEBHOOK_URL, otherwise Google login/redirects may fail.  ￼

My recommendation

Use n8n Cloud first for testing.
Move to self-hosted n8n later when the workflow is stable and you want lower cost/control.

content: [Fri, May 15, 2026 at 02:01 PM]

[Fri, May 15, 2026 at 02:01 Best answer: use both

For your app, the best method is:

Task	Best Tool
Rename/save student files in Drive	Google Apps Script
Read Google Classroom courses/assignments	Google Apps Script
Return marks to Classroom	Google Apps Script / Classroom API
AI OCR, marking, QC, PDF workflow	n8n
Connecting Gemini + ChatGPT + Supabase	n8n

Google Apps Script has direct Classroom access through the Classroom advanced service/API.  ￼
n8n is better for multi-step AI workflows and Google Drive automation.  ￼

My recommendation

Use Google Apps Script first to test whether your university allows:

1. reading Classroom assignments
2. accessing submitted files
3. updating grades
4. returning work

Then use n8n only after that works.

Simple architecture

Google Classroom
      ↓
Google Apps Script
      ↓
Google Drive Folder + Google Sheet
      ↓
n8n AI Workflow
      ↓
Marked PDF + Score
      ↓
Google Apps Script sends mark back to Classroom

Why this is best

Google Apps Script is better for Classroom because it lives inside Google Workspace.

n8n is better for the AI marking pipeline because it is easier to debug, branch, retry, and connect to Gemini/OpenAI/Supabase.

So don’t choose one only.

Use Apps Script for Google Classroom.
Use n8n for AI automation.

snapshots.content: [Fri, May 15, 2026 at 02:01 PM]

content:
[Fri, May 15, 2026 at 02:30 PM]

Use n8n because your workflow is not just Google Classroom.

It includes:

Classroom → Drive → OCR → AI marking → QC → PDF report → marks → Classroom

Why n8n helps

Reason	Why it matters
Visual workflow	You can see each step and where it fails.
Connects many services	Gemini, OpenAI, Supabase, Drive, email, webhooks, APIs.
Retries failed steps	Useful when OCR or AI calls fail.
Better for AI pipelines	Easier to chain Marking Agent → QC Agent → PDF Agent.
Webhook support	Your app can press one button and trigger the whole workflow.
Logs	You can inspect what happened for each student.

Apps Script alone is weaker for AI

Google Apps Script is good for:

Classroom + Drive + Sheets

But it becomes harder for:

OCR + multiple AI agents + retries + Supabase + PDF generation + error tracking

Best use

Use:

Google Apps Script = Classroom access
n8n = AI workflow engine
Supabase = database/storage
Your app = teacher interface

So n8n is not replacing Apps Script.
It is the control centre for the AI marking process.

content:
[Fri, May 15, 2026 at 02:35 PM]

No — for your use case, basic n8n setup is not hard.

The difficult part is usually:

* Google OAuth permissions
* AI workflow design
* reliable OCR/marking logic

—not installing n8n itself.

Your easiest path

Option 1 — BEST FOR YOU

Use n8n Cloud￼

This avoids:

* servers
* Linux setup
* Docker
* SSL certificates
* reverse proxies

You simply:

1. create account
2. build workflows in browser
3. connect Google account
4. connect Gemini/OpenAI keys

Setup time:

* 1–3 hours for first working workflow
* 1–2 weeks for polished AI marking pipeline

⸻

What your first workflow should be

Do NOT start with AI marking.

Start with:

Google Classroom
    ↓
Get student submissions
    ↓
Rename files
    ↓
Save to Drive folder

If this works:

* your university permissions work
* Classroom API works
* Drive access works

THEN add AI.

⸻

Your likely workflow later

Webhook Trigger
    ↓
Receive assignment/script/rubric
    ↓
Save to Drive
    ↓
Gemini OCR
    ↓
Marking Agent
    ↓
QC Agent
    ↓
Generate LaTeX
    ↓
Compile PDF
    ↓
Return score to Classroom

⸻

What WILL be challenging

1. Google Classroom permissions

Your university may block:

* grade updates
* submission access
* API scopes

This is the biggest unknown.

⸻

2. OCR consistency

Handwriting quality varies.

You already noticed:

* sometimes perfect
* sometimes terrible

You’ll need:

* retries
* evidence checking
* QC verification

⸻

3. PDF/LaTeX reliability

This is where many systems fail.

Your best approach:

* structured JSON output from AI
* THEN generate LaTeX from templates

NOT:

AI writes full LaTeX from scratch every time

⸻

My recommendation

Phase 1 (This week)

Build ONLY:

Classroom → Drive rename/save

using:

* Google Apps Script
* or n8n + Drive

⸻

Phase 2

Add:

* Gemini OCR
* extracted text viewer

⸻

Phase 3

Add:

* marking
* rubric
* QC
* totals

⸻

Phase 4

Add:

* LaTeX
* PDF
* return grades

⸻

Final recommendation

For you:

Tool	Use
Google Apps Script	Classroom access
n8n Cloud	Workflow automation
Gemini	OCR
ChatGPT/Claude	QC + report generation
Supabase	storage/database
Overleaf/PDF service	typesetting

That is a strong architecture for your app.

snapshots.content: [Fri, May 15, 2026 at 02:35 PM]

title: Your UI is looking much more structured now.
content:
[Fri, May 15, 2026 at 09:06 PM]

Your UI is looking much more structured now. A few things stand out immediately from this screenshot of your Tasks & Annotations workflow:

What is working well

Strong visual identity

The dark academic/dashboard aesthetic is very distinctive. It already feels more like a premium education platform than a generic task app.

Good separation of areas

You now clearly have:

* Lessons
* Knowledge Base
* Library
* GitHub
* Tasks & Annotations

That architecture makes sense for your AI lesson + marking ecosystem.

Task cards feel “actionable”

The cards already look like:

* assignments,
* marking jobs,
* lesson generators,
* or workflow pipelines.

That is important psychologically because users feel like work is progressing through a system.

⸻

Biggest improvements I would make next

1. Add AI workflow status directly on the card

Right now the card looks like a normal task manager.

Your app becomes special when the AI pipeline becomes visible.

For example:

Stage	Status
OCR Extraction	✅
Question Detection	✅
Marking	🔄
QC Audit	⏳
PDF Typesetting	⏳

This would transform the card from “task” → “AI marking job”.

⸻

2. Add upload zones directly on cards

Currently “Attach” is small and easy to miss.

Instead add:

* 📄 Upload Question Paper
* 📝 Upload Student Script
* 📋 Upload Rubric

directly inside expanded cards.

That matches the workflow you described previously.

⸻

3. Your cards need a “Generate Output” section

After processing:

* View Marked PDF
* View Rubric
* View QC Report
* Send to Classroom
* Export to Library

This is where the app becomes a complete marking ecosystem.

⸻

4. Add confidence warnings

This is extremely important for trust.

Example:

* OCR Confidence: 98%
* Question Match Confidence: 91%
* QC Flag: Possible missing page

This alone would massively reduce hallucination concerns.

⸻

Most important architectural recommendation

You asked earlier about n8n.

Looking at this UI now:

Yes — n8n fits your app extremely well.

Your app is no longer “just a website.”
It is becoming an orchestration platform.

You have:

* uploads,
* OCR,
* marking,
* QC,
* LaTeX,
* PDF generation,
* Google Classroom sync,
* notifications,
* storage,
* RAG ingestion,
* YouTube scraping,
* publishing.

That is exactly what n8n is good at orchestrating.

⸻

Recommended architecture for your app

Frontend (Bolt)
        ↓
Supabase DB + Storage
        ↓
n8n Orchestrator
        ↓
AI Agents
 ├── OCR Agent
 ├── Marking Agent
 ├── QC Agent
 ├── Lesson Generator
 ├── Diagram Generator
 ├── LaTeX Builder
 └── PDF Compiler
        ↓
Outputs
 ├── Library
 ├── Google Classroom
 ├── Drive
 └── Student Feedback

⸻

What I would build NEXT if this were my roadmap

Phase 1 — Immediate

Highest impact

1. Expandable task cards
2. Dedicated upload slots
3. AI status stages
4. Progress logs
5. PDF preview modal

⸻

Phase 2 — Core automation

1. n8n orchestration
2. Supabase triggers
3. Gemini OCR
4. QC pipeline
5. Auto PDF compile

⸻

Phase 3 — Premium features

1. AI moderation/QC confidence
2. Classroom sync
3. RAG lesson verification
4. Auto YouTube resources
5. Interactive diagrams

⸻

One UI improvement that would dramatically help

Add a floating “Pipeline Console”

Something like:

[ OCR ] → [ Marking ] → [ QC ] → [ LaTeX ] → [ PDF ]

with live logs underneath.

This would make the app feel like:

* GitHub Actions,
* Cursor AI,
* Claude Code,
* or a professional AI IDE.

That would strongly differentiate your platform from ordinary education apps.

⸻

Overall assessment

You are no longer building:

* a lesson generator,
* or a marking tool.

You are building:

an AI academic operating system.

The structure is already visible in this screenshot.

content:
[Sat, May 16, 2026, 02:04 AM]

Yes — for your AI marking system, batch marking is much better than marking one script at a time.

Especially for your planned workflow with:

n8n

[Supabase](https://supabase.com?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Gemini OCR

QC agents

LaTeX reports

Google Classroom integration



---

Why Batch Marking Is Better

1. Much Lower AI Costs

Instead of:

Upload → OCR → AI → QC → PDF

for every student individually,

you process:

one assignment paper

one rubric

many student scripts


This avoids repeatedly:

parsing the same rubric

reading the same assignment

rebuilding prompts


That saves a lot of tokens and API calls.


---

2. Better Consistency

This is one of the biggest advantages.

When scripts are marked in batches:

the rubric stays “hot”

standards remain consistent

mark allocation becomes more stable


Your current issue:

> same script sometimes receives different scores



Batch processing helps reduce that problem.


---

3. Faster Workflow

Instead of:

opening workflows repeatedly


You can:

Assignment Folder
        ↓
n8n watches folder
        ↓
Processes ALL scripts
        ↓
Outputs reports

Much cleaner.


---

4. Easier QC

Your QC agent can compare:

all scripts

all totals

all rubric usage


This allows:

anomaly detection

missing question checks

outlier score detection


Example:

> “Why did Student B lose marks here while Student A got full marks for similar work?”



Very powerful.


---

5. Better for Your University Workflow

Your actual use case is probably:

20–100 scripts
same assignment
same rubric
same marking scheme

That is exactly where batch automation shines.


---

Recommended Architecture For You

BEST APPROACH

Assignment-Level Batch Pipeline

Assignment Folder
    ├── Question Paper
    ├── Rubric
    └── Student Scripts

Then:

n8n Trigger
        ↓
OCR Extraction
        ↓
Evidence Extraction
        ↓
Marking Agent
        ↓
QC Agent
        ↓
LaTeX Report Builder
        ↓
PDF Export
        ↓
Upload Results
        ↓
Return Grades


---

Smart Design Decision

Separate:

Assignment Context

from

Student Processing

This is critical.


---

Example

Process Once:

rubric

marking scheme

assignment questions

solutions


Store in:

Supabase

vector DB

cached context


Then every student script references the same context.

This massively improves:

speed

consistency

cost



---

Your Ideal Data Structure

Batch Object

{
  "course": "ENGR405",
  "assignment": "Signals Quiz 2",
  "rubric": "...",
  "questions": "...",
  "students": [
    "student1.pdf",
    "student2.pdf",
    "student3.pdf"
  ]
}


---

VERY IMPORTANT

Do NOT send all scripts to AI at once.

Bad idea.

Instead:

Use:

“Batch orchestration”

Meaning:

same assignment context

individual student marking jobs


So:

Shared Rubric
        ↓
Student A → Mark
Student B → Mark
Student C → Mark

This prevents:

token overload

hallucinations

context collapse



---

Your Best First n8n Batch Workflow

Version 1

Google Drive Folder Trigger
        ↓
Detect Assignment Folder
        ↓
Load Rubric
        ↓
Loop Through Student PDFs
        ↓
OCR
        ↓
Mark
        ↓
Save JSON

Do NOT generate PDFs yet.

First:

prove marking works reliably


Then:

add LaTeX

add QC

add Classroom upload



---

Best Long-Term Strategy

Stage	Goal

Stage 1	Batch file organization
Stage 2	OCR extraction
Stage 3	AI marking
Stage 4	QC validation
Stage 5	PDF generation
Stage 6	Classroom integration


That progression gives you the highest chance of building a stable system.

title: OpenRouter Owl Alpha Pricing￼
content:
[Sun, May 17, 2026 at 07:51 AM]
Yes — surprisingly cheap right now.

According to  OpenRouter Owl Alpha Pricing￼, Owl Alpha is currently listed as free on OpenRouter, with:

* $0 input tokens
* $0 output tokens
* about a 1 million token context window  ￼

That’s why many developers are experimenting with it for:

* coding agents
* automation workflows
* long-document processing
* multi-agent systems

The Catch

The free access usually comes with limits such as:

* request caps
* queue delays
* possible throttling during busy times
* no guaranteed uptime  ￼

OpenRouter’s free tier commonly has limits like:

* ~50 requests/day for free accounts
* rate limits per minute  ￼

So for:

* testing
* prototyping
* experimenting with your marking workflow

…it’s extremely attractive.

But for:

* production university marking
* hundreds of scripts
* guaranteed reliability

…you would probably eventually move to:

* paid OpenRouter credits
* Gemini
* Claude
* OpenAI
* self-hosted models

Why This Matters for You

Your system could benefit massively because your workflows are very context-heavy:

Assignment Paper
+ Rubric
+ Student Scripts
+ QC Instructions
+ LaTeX Templates
+ Audit Rules

Most models become expensive with huge contexts.

Owl Alpha currently gives you:

* very large context
* tool use
* agent workflows
* low/no experimentation cost

which is unusual right now.

Important Warning

OpenRouter notes that prompts/completions for Owl Alpha may be logged by providers.  ￼

So I would avoid sending:

* confidential student records
* grades
* private institutional data

until you have a secure/private deployment strategy.

My View for Your Project

For your current stage:

* prototyping
* workflow testing
* agent orchestration experiments

Owl Alpha is probably one of the cheapest powerful options available right now.

For final deployment:

* Gemini for OCR
* OpenAI/Claude for QC
* Owl Alpha for orchestration
* n8n for automation

could be a very strong combination.

content:
[Sun, May 17, 2026 at 10:42 AM]

Best first method: Google Drive folder route

Google Classroom saves student submissions into a Drive folder for that assignment. The Classroom API also exposes a studentWorkFolder for assignment submissions.  ￼

Step 1 — Open the assignment folder

1. Open Google Classroom
2. Click the class
3. Click Classwork
4. Open the assignment
5. Click the Drive folder / assignment folder
6. You should see the student files

Google says students attach work through Classroom, and teachers can view/grade returned work in Classroom/Drive.  ￼

⸻

Step 2 — Download the files manually first

In the assignment Drive folder:

1. Select all student scripts
2. Right-click
3. Choose Download
4. Google Drive downloads them as a .zip
5. Unzip the folder on your laptop

Rename files like:

Topic_StudentName_Script.pdf

Example:

SolarPanelSystem_AmoryBennett_Script.pdf
SolarPanelSystem_Assignment.pdf
SolarPanelSystem_Rubric.pdf

⸻

Step 3 — Upload into your app

In your app’s to-do card:

1. Open the assessment card
2. Upload:
    * Assessment Paper
    * Rubric
    * Student Script(s)
3. Click Analyse / Mark
4. App sends files to:
    * OCR
    * marking agent
    * QC agent
    * PDF report generator

⸻

Better workflow for your app

Google Classroom
   ↓
Assignment Drive Folder
   ↓
Download ZIP
   ↓
Rename Files
   ↓
Upload to To-Do Card
   ↓
OCR + Marking + QC
   ↓
Marked PDF + Score

⸻

Later automation

Once the manual version works, automate with:

Task	Tool
Find assignment	Google Classroom API
Get student submissions	Classroom API studentSubmissions.list
Get attached files	Google Drive API
Rename files	n8n / Google Apps Script
Upload to your app	Supabase storage API
Mark scripts	Gemini + Owl/OpenAI
Return marks	Classroom API

The Classroom API has a studentSubmissions.list endpoint for retrieving submissions you’re allowed to view.  ￼

title: n8n only when the manual workflow works.
content:
[Sun, May 17, 2026 at 10:52 Yes — put the system in place first, then pay for n8n only when the manual workflow works.

Step 1 — Create your folder structure

In Google Drive create:

AI Marking System
├── 01_Assignments
├── 02_Rubrics
├── 03_Student_Scripts
├── 04_OCR_Output
├── 05_Marked_JSON
├── 06_QC_Checked
├── 07_Final_PDFs
└── 08_Returned_To_Classroom

⸻

Step 2 — Use a strict file naming rule

Use:

Course_Topic_StudentName_Type.pdf

Examples:

ENGR440_SolarPanelSystem_Assignment.pdf
ENGR440_SolarPanelSystem_Rubric.pdf
ENGR440_SolarPanelSystem_AmoryBennett_Script.pdf

⸻

Step 3 — Build your manual test workflow

Before n8n, test this manually:

Google Classroom
↓
Download assignment folder
↓
Rename files
↓
Upload assignment + rubric + 1 script into your app
↓
Run OCR
↓
Mark
↓
QC check
↓
Generate PDF report

Your target is:

One student script marked correctly from start to finish.

⸻

Step 4 — Create a tracking spreadsheet

Columns:

Job ID	Course	Topic	Student	Assignment	Rubric	Script	OCR Status	Marking Status	Score	QC Status	PDF

This becomes your dashboard before automation.

⸻

Step 5 — Prepare your app upload screen

Your app should accept:

File Type	Required?
Assignment Paper	Yes
Rubric	Yes
Student Script(s)	Yes
Extra supporting file	Optional

And the app should label files automatically as:

Assignment
Rubric
Script

⸻

Step 6 — Test with 3 scripts

Do not start with a whole class.

Test:

1 neat script
1 average script
1 messy script

Check:

* Did OCR read the answer correctly?
* Did marking follow the rubric?
* Were marks lost clearly shown?
* Did QC catch errors?
* Was the final score correct?

⸻

Step 7 — Only then choose n8n

n8n Cloud pricing is based on workflow executions; the official pricing page says all plans include unlimited users, workflows, and integrations, with execution-based monthly limits.  ￼

You can also self-host n8n using Docker, which n8n recommends for most self-hosting setups.  ￼

My recommendation

Start with free/manual + Google Drive + spreadsheet.

Then move to:

Manual test → Google Apps Script → Self-hosted n8n → n8n Cloud

Do not buy n8n until your app can reliably mark 3 scripts manually.

Google Classroom → Drive → AI Marking App → n8n Automation

This sequence is designed specifically for your marking/QC platform.

⸻

* question paper PDF

⸻

Upload:

ENGR440_W1L1_Rubric.pdf

⸻

Step 6 — Configure Student Submission

Set:

* students can upload PDFs/photos
* due date
* points
* topic

⸻

PHASE 3 — STUDENT SUBMISSIONS

Step 7 — Students Upload Scripts

Students:

* complete assignment
* upload scanned PDF/photos

⸻

Step 8 — Open Assignment Folder in Drive

When submissions arrive:

1. Open assignment
2. Click Drive folder icon
3. Open student submissions folder

⸻

PHASE 4 — DOWNLOAD & ORGANIZE FILES

Step 9 — Download Student Scripts

Select all:

* scripts
* assignment
* rubric

Then:

* right-click
* Download ZIP

⸻

Step 10 — Extract ZIP

Unzip locally on laptop.

⸻

Step 11 — Rename Files Properly

Use strict naming:

Course_Topic_StudentName_Type.pdf

Examples:

ENGR440_W1L1_AmoryBennett_Script.pdf
ENGR440_W1L1_Assignment.pdf
ENGR440_W1L1_Rubric.pdf

⸻

PHASE 5 — PREPARE YOUR APP

Step 12 — Create Upload Area in App

Your app should support:

File	Required
Assignment	Yes
Rubric	Yes
Student Script(s)	Yes

⸻

Step 13 — Add File Classification

When uploaded:

Assignment
Rubric
Script

should be auto-detected.

⸻

PHASE 6 — OCR PIPELINE

Step 14 — Send Scripts to OCR

Recommended:

OCR Tool	Use
Google Gemini￼	Handwriting + math
Mistral OCR￼	Fast document parsing

⸻

Step 15 — OCR Output Must Become Structured JSON

NOT plain text.

Example:

{
  "student": "Amory Bennett",
  "question": "Q1(a)",
  "answer": "Voltage = IR ..."
}

Save outputs in:

04_OCR_Output

⸻

PHASE 7 — MARKING ENGINE

Step 16 — Send OCR + Rubric + Assignment to AI

Send:

* assignment
* rubric
* extracted answers

to:

* Owl Alpha
* OpenAI
* Gemini

⸻

Step 17 — Force Structured Marking Output

Example:

{
  "question": "Q1(a)",
  "marks_awarded": 3,
  "marks_possible": 5,
  "errors": [
    "Incorrect formula"
  ],
  "feedback": "Correct method but arithmetic error"
}

Save to:

05_Marked_JSON

⸻

PHASE 8 — QC AGENT

Step 18 — Run QC Verification

QC checks:

* missing questions
* incorrect totals
* hallucinations
* rubric mismatch
* arithmetic

Save to:

06_QC_Checked

⸻

PHASE 9 — PDF GENERATION

Step 19 — Generate Final PDF Report

Create:

* student score
* marks lost
* corrections
* examiner comments
* strengths/weaknesses

Save PDFs in:

07_Final_PDFs

⸻

PHASE 10 — RETURN TO CLASSROOM

Step 20 — Upload Feedback Back to Classroom

Manual initially:

1. Open Classroom
2. Open student
3. Attach marked PDF
4. Enter grade
5. Return

⸻

PHASE 11 — TRACKING SYSTEM

Step 21 — Create Spreadsheet Dashboard

Columns:

Job ID	Student	OCR	Marking	QC	Score	Returned

This becomes your audit trail.

⸻

PHASE 12 — ONLY NOW ADD n8n

After manual system works.

⸻

PHASE 13 — INSTALL n8n

Use:

n8n Installation Guide￼

⸻

PHASE 14 — AUTOMATE THE PIPELINE

n8n workflow:

Google Classroom Trigger
        ↓
Download Submission
        ↓
Rename Files
        ↓
Upload to Supabase
        ↓
OCR
        ↓
Marking
        ↓
QC
        ↓
Generate PDF
        ↓
Return Marks

⸻

PHASE 15 — ADD ADVANCED FEATURES LATER

Later add:

* Hermes agents
* batch marking
* retry logic
* automatic QC escalation
* RAG verification
* auto-LaTeX typesetting
* analytics dashboard

⸻

YOUR MOST IMPORTANT GOAL

Before ANY automation:

Successfully mark ONE script end-to-end reliably.

Then:

* 3 scripts
* 10 scripts
* full class
* batch automation

That sequence will save you months of frustration.

title: Rubric
content:
[Mon, May 18, 2026 at 10:20 AM]

One-Page AI Marking Rubric System

Goal

When an assignment is created inside the app, the system should automatically generate:

1. The Assignment Paper
2. A Matching One-Page Rubric
3. Question-by-question mark allocations
4. Expected answers / marking points
5. Total marks automatically calculated

The rubric becomes the official marking guide used later by the AI marking agent.

⸻

Design Requirements

The rubric MUST:

* Be ONLY one standard page
* Be compact and table-based
* Contain all questions
* Show:
    * question number
    * expected answer / marking points
    * marks available
    * marks awarded
    * errors
    * corrections
* Automatically calculate totals
* Be easy to:
    * display in app
    * export to PDF
    * upload to Classroom
    * read on mobile

⸻

Workflow Inside the App

Assignment Creation Stage

When lecturer clicks:

“Generate Assignment”

the system automatically creates:

Generated Item	Purpose
Assignment Paper	Student version
One-Page Rubric	AI marking guide
Answer Key	Expected solutions
Mark Allocation Table	Marks per question

⸻

One-Page Rubric Layout

Q#	Expected Answer / Key Points	Common Errors	Correction	Marks	Awarded
1	Correct ROI formula	Wrong denominator	Use investment cost	5	___
2	Correct IRR explanation	Missing interpretation	Explain profitability	4	___
3	Correct break-even formula	Arithmetic error	Correct calculation = 45 units	6	___

⸻

Bottom Summary Section

Section	Score
Section A	___ / 30
Section B	___ / 50
Presentation	___ / 20

FINAL TOTAL: ___ / 100

FINAL PERCENTAGE: ___ %

⸻

AI Marking Rules

1. Mark ONLY visible student work.
2. DO NOT hallucinate missing answers.
3. If blank:
    * “No response provided.”
4. If unclear:
    * “Unclear from script.”
5. Keep corrections short.
6. Keep rubric compact.
7. No large reports.
8. No essay feedback.
9. One-page maximum.
10. Optimised for fast review.

⸻

Benefits for Your App

✅ Faster marking
✅ Easier QC checking
✅ Lower token/API cost
✅ Easier Classroom upload
✅ Easier student feedback
✅ Cleaner PDF exports
✅ Better mobile display
✅ Faster lecturer review
✅ Easier database storage
✅ Consistent marking format

Answer Key	Expected solutions
Mark Allocation Table	Marks per question

⸻

One-Page Rubric Layout

Q#	Expected Answer / Key Points	Common Errors	Correction	Marks	Awarded
1	Correct ROI formula	Wrong denominator	Use investment cost	5	___
2	Correct IRR explanation	Missing interpretation	Explain profitability	4	___
3	Correct break-even formula	Arithmetic error	Correct calculation = 45 units	6	___

⸻

Bottom Summary Section

Section	Score
Section A	___ / 30
Section B	___ / 50
Presentation	___ / 20

FINAL TOTAL: ___ / 100

FINAL PERCENTAGE: ___ %

⸻

AI Marking Rules

1. Mark ONLY visible student work.
2. DO NOT hallucinate missing answers.
3. If blank:
    * “No response provided.”
4. If unclear:
    * “Unclear from script.”
5. Keep corrections short.
6. Keep rubric compact.
7. No large reports.
8. No essay feedback.
9. One-page maximum.
10. Optimised for fast review.

⸻

Benefits for Your App

✅ Faster marking
✅ Easier QC checking
✅ Lower token/API cost
✅ Easier Classroom upload
✅ Easier student feedback
✅ Cleaner PDF exports
✅ Better mobile display
✅ Faster lecturer review
✅ Easier database storage
✅ Consistent marking format

For your app workflow, the best method is to use a two-stage rubric system:

title: Google Classroom's API capabilities
content:
[Mon, May 18, 2026, 08:06 AM]

This is a question about Google Classroom's API capabilities, not a build task. Let me explain the options:

Google Classroom API can create assignments programmatically, including attaching links, Drive files, and YouTube videos. To do this from the app you'd need:

Google OAuth — the user signs in with their Google account and grants classroom.coursework.me + classroom.courses.readonly scopes
A Bolt Database edge function that calls the Classroom REST API to create the assignment with the title, description, and any links/attachments from the card
Course ID mapping — the first time, the user picks which Classroom course matches ENGR440, and the app remembers that mapping
What can be sent:

Assignment title + description (from the note field)
Links (any URLs in the note)
Due date (if set on the card)
Attached PDFs — only if they're in Google Drive; Classroom API doesn't accept arbitrary file uploads directly
What can't be sent directly:

Files stored in Bolt Database storage — these would need to be a shared link, attached as a URL material rather than a Drive file
The realistic scope of work:

Google OAuth integration (new)
Edge function calling classroom.googleapis.com
A one-time course-code → Classroom course ID mapping UI
The button triggers the flow and shows success/error feedback
This is a moderate-sized feature. Want me to build it? If so, confirm you have a Google Cloud project where you can create OAuth credentials, or I can walk you through that step first.

title: Export Schedule to To-Do List
content:
[Tue, May 19, 2026 at 08:00 AM]

Add an “Export Schedule to To-Do List” icon button to the Schedule page.

Place the icon button in the schedule header/top action bar near the existing schedule controls.

Button requirements:

- Icon: use an export/send/checklist style icon

- Tooltip text: “Export Schedule to To-Do List”

- Label on larger screens: “Export to To-Do”

- On mobile, show icon only

- Button should be clearly visible but not crowded

When clicked:

1. Read the complete current schedule.

2. For every schedule row, create a matching To-Do List card.

3. Each To-Do card should include:

   - Course code

   - Week number

   - Lesson number

   - Date

   - Topic/title

   - Source: Schedule Export

   - Status: Not Started

   - Actions:

     - Generate Lesson

     - Open Lesson Files

     - Create Student Script PDF

     - Create Rubric

     - Mark Scripts

To-Do card title format:

[Course Code] - W[Week#]L[Lesson#] - [Topic]

Example:

ENGR405 - W2L3 - Amplitude Modulation

Prevent duplicates:

- Before creating a To-Do card, check whether a card already exists with the same course code, week number, lesson number, date, and topic.

- If it already exists, skip it.

- After export, show a summary message:

  “Export complete: X cards created, Y duplicates skipped.”

Add loading feedback:

- While exporting, show:

  “Exporting schedule to To-Do List...”

- Disable the button during export.

- Show success or error toast when finished.

Important:

- Do not delete or modify the original schedule.

- Do not overwrite existing To-Do cards.

- Preserve the schedule order when creating To-Do cards.

- The exported cards should appear in the To-Do List in the same order as the schedule.
———-

Best button placement

Put it here:

Schedule Page
[Add Lesson] [Import CSV] [Export to To-Do 📤]

This lets the schedule become your planning source, and the To-Do List becomes your work/action dashboard.

[Course Code] - W[Week#]L[Lesson#] - [Topic]

Example:

ENGR405 - W2L3 - Amplitude Modulation

Prevent duplicates:

- Before creating a To-Do card, check whether a card already exists with the same course code, week number, lesson number, date, and topic.

- If it already exists, skip it.

- After export, show a summary message:

  “Export complete: X cards created, Y duplicates skipped.”

Add loading feedback:

- While exporting, show:

  “Exporting schedule to To-Do List...”

- Disable the button during export.

- Show success or error toast when finished.

Important:

- Do not delete or modify the original schedule.

- Do not overwrite existing To-Do cards.

- Preserve the schedule order when creating To-Do cards.

- The exported cards should appear in the To-Do List in the same order as the schedule.
———-

Best button placement

Put it here:

title: To tie your app into Google Classroom properly, the best architecture is:
content:
[Tue, May 19, 2026 at 08:04 AM]

To tie your app into Google Classroom properly, the best architecture is:

Recommended Workflow

Schedule
   ↓
Export to To-Do List
   ↓
Generate Lesson + Assignment + Rubric
   ↓
Push Assignment to Google Classroom
   ↓
Students Submit Scripts
   ↓
Download Scripts Automatically
   ↓
AI Marking + QC
   ↓
Return Marks + Feedback to Classroom

⸻

What You Need

1. Google Cloud Project

Go to:

Google Cloud Console￼

Create:

* New Project
* Enable APIs

Enable these APIs:

* Google Classroom API
* Google Drive API
* Google Docs API
* Google Sheets API

⸻

2. OAuth Login

Your app must allow:

Sign in with Google

Use:

* OAuth 2.0
* Google Identity Services

When the teacher logs in:

* your app receives permission
* your app can access:
    * Classroom
    * Drive
    * Assignments
    * Student submissions

⸻

3. Required Google Scopes

Add scopes like:

https://www.googleapis.com/auth/classroom.courses
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/classroom.coursework.students
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/classroom.student-submissions.students.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive

⸻

4. New “Classroom” Section in Your App

Add a sidebar item:

📚 Classroom

Inside it:

Feature	Purpose
Sync Classes	Pull teacher classes
Sync Assignments	Pull assignments
Create Assignment	Push work to Classroom
Download Scripts	Pull student uploads
Return Grades	Send marks back
Classroom Settings	Connect courses

⸻

5. Connect Schedule → Classroom

When exporting schedule to To-Do:

Add:

Linked Classroom Course
Assignment Type
Due Date
Points
Topic

Example:

Schedule Topic	Classroom Assignment
Modulation & Demodulation	ENGR405 Assignment
Improper Integrals	MATH272 Homework

⸻

6. Add “Push to Classroom” Button

Each To-Do card should have:

[Generate Lesson]
[Create Assignment]
[Push to Classroom]

⸻

7. What Happens When Clicked

Your app should:

Generate:

* Assignment PDF
* Rubric PDF
* Instructions
* Optional Canva instructions

Upload to:

* Google Classroom
* Google Drive

Create:

* Coursework Assignment

Students then submit directly in Classroom.

⸻

8. Student Script Download Automation

This is the BIG one.

When students submit:

Your app should:

* detect submissions
* download all files
* rename files automatically

Example:

ENGR405-W2L3-JohnBrown-Script.pdf

Question paper:

ENGR405-W2L3-Assignment.pdf

Rubric:

ENGR405-W2L3-Rubric.pdf

⸻

9. Folder Structure (VERY IMPORTANT)

In Google Drive create:

/AI_MARKING_SYSTEM
    /ENGR405
        /Assignments
        /Rubrics
        /StudentScripts
        /MarkedScripts
        /Feedback

This becomes your pipeline.

⸻

10. Add “Mark Scripts” Button

To-Do card:

[Download Scripts]
[Mark Scripts]
[QC Audit]
[Return Marks]

⸻

11. AI Marking Flow

Your app should process:

Assignment
+
Rubric
+
Student Script

Then produce:

* marked script PDF
* rubric breakdown
* lost marks
* corrections
* totals
* percentage
* teacher feedback

Exactly as you wanted earlier.

⸻

12. Return Marks to Classroom

Use Classroom API:

courses.courseWork.studentSubmissions.patch

Your app can:

* upload feedback PDF
* return grade
* mark as returned

Student instantly sees:

* score
* feedback
* marked script

⸻

13. Best Automation Stack

For YOUR app specifically:

Tool	Role
n8n￼	automation/orchestration
Supabase￼	database + storage
Google Classroom API￼	coursework
Google Drive API￼	files
Gemini API￼	OCR + marking
OpenAI API￼	QC auditing
Overleaf￼	LaTeX compile
Bolt.new￼	frontend

⸻

Suggested App Flow

Schedule Page

[Export to To-Do]

↓

To-Do Card

[Generate Lesson]
[Create Assignment]
[Push to Classroom]

↓

Classroom Pipeline

Students Submit

↓

AI Pipeline

[Download Scripts]
[Mark]
[QC]
[Generate PDFs]
[Return Marks]

⸻

MOST IMPORTANT RECOMMENDATION

Do NOT try to automate everything immediately.

Phase 1

* Export schedule → To-Do
* Push assignment to Classroom

Phase 2

* Download student submissions automatically

Phase 3

* AI marking pipeline

Phase 4

* Return grades automatically

That staged approach will massively reduce complexity and debugging problems.

title: Realistic implementation time
content:
[Tue, May 19, 2026 at 08:07 AM]

Realistic implementation time

Phase	Feature	Time
1	Schedule → To-Do export icon	1–2 days
2	To-Do card → assignment/rubric generation	2–4 days
3	Google login + permissions	2–5 days
4	Push assignment to Google Classroom	3–7 days
5	Download student scripts from Classroom/Drive	5–10 days
6	Rename and organise files	2–4 days
7	AI marking + rubric feedback PDF	1–3 weeks
8	Return marks/feedback to Classroom	3–7 days
9	Testing and fixing edge cases	1–2 weeks

Total estimate

Basic working version: about 2–3 weeks

Reliable classroom-ready version: about 6–8 weeks

Full polished system with marking, QC, PDFs, Drive folders, and returned grades: about 8–12 weeks

Best first build

Start with this:

Schedule → To-Do List → Generate Assignment + Rubric → Push to Google Classroom

That part could be working in 2–3 weeks.

Then add:

Download scripts → Mark scripts → Return marks

That is the harder part.
